comments:
Summary: This paper proposes "morph-fitting", a resource-light post-processing method that fine-tunes pre-trained word vectors using simple language-specific morphological rules. The method creates ATTRACT constraints (inflectional synonyms) and REPEL constraints (derivational antonyms) from heuristic rules in four languages (EN, DE, IT, RU) and applies the ATTRACT-REPEL algorithm to specialise vectors. The paper reports consistent gains on intrinsic benchmarks (SimLex-999, SimVerb-3500, multilingual SimLex) and shows improvements in an extrinsic downstream task (Dialogue State Tracking), especially for morphologically rich languages.

Strengths: The idea is simple, practical and portable: it does not rely on curated lexica and can be applied to multiple languages. Experiments are fairly comprehensive (multiple languages, vector initialisations, intrinsic and extrinsic evaluations). Comparison to other post-processing methods (retrofitting, counter-fitting) is included and ATTRACT-REPEL based morph-fitting shows superior performance. The authors acknowledge limitations (noisy constraints) and provide language rules as supplemental material which aids reproducibility.

Weaknesses and suggestions: The rule-based constraints are admittedly heuristic and sometimes noisy; more analysis of the impact of noisy or incorrect constraints would be useful (e.g., ablation removing noisy rules, or precision/recall of extracted constraints against a gold lexicon where available). The paper would benefit from statistical significance testing of reported gains and a deeper quantitative analysis of improvements for low-frequency words (beyond illustrative examples). The discrepancy between intrinsic gains and downstream DST performance is noted but could be further explored (e.g., diagnostics to understand which slots/values or utterance types benefit most). Finally, evaluation on additional morphologically rich languages or experiments using gold morphological lexicons (where available) would strengthen generalisability claims.

Overall assessment: This is a well-motivated, well-executed piece of work that makes a useful contribution to the semantic specialisation literature by exploiting simple morphological signals. It balances novelty and practicality and should be of interest to researchers working on word representations and multilingual NLP. With additional analysis on noisy constraints and more diagnostic evaluations, the contribution could be made even stronger.
